title: Solo Dev Built a Crypto Trading Bot — Just Hit $1,000+ in Public Paper Trading
author: Agile_Strategy_223

comments.author: Denis_Kurilchik
comments.body:
paper trading milestones are worth tracking but the number to watch is the same performance at 10-20% of live contract sizes. the transition from paper to real money usually reveals fill issues and slippage that sim doesn't capture. $1000 on paper with a 70% win rate can look very different at first real-money sizing.

what's the strategy type? crypto momentum, mean-reversion, something else?

replies.author: Agile_Strategy_223
replies.body:
That’s a very fair point and honestly one of the reasons I’ve been trying to stay realistic about how I present the paper trading side.  
Right now I view paper trading primarily as:  
execution validation  
onboarding/testing  
strategy behavior analysis  
UX feedback  
risk-flow simulation  
…more than “proof of guaranteed profitability.”  
I completely agree that once real capital enters the equation, things like:  
slippage  
liquidity depth  
spread changes  
partial fills  
volatility spikes  
emotional behavior  
API latency  
start changing the picture very quickly.  
That’s also why I’ve been focusing heavily on gradual scaling and risk controls instead of trying to market unrealistic outcomes.  
On the strategy side, the architecture is multi-strategy rather than relying on one model.  
Current core strategies include:  
✅ AI Weighted / Balanced  
A blended multi-factor scoring approach using momentum, confidence weighting, and risk filters.  
✅ Mean Reversion  
More conservative rebound-focused entries after overextensions.  
✅ Momentum / Growth  
Trend-following and strength continuation logic.  
✅ Volume Spike / Aggressive  
Higher-volatility breakout detection and rapid movement opportunities.  
The system also layers:  
trailing stops  
position sizing controls  
confidence scoring  
risk weighting  
automated exits  
portfolio allocation logic  
Most of the stronger recent testing has been on crypto because the market structure and API accessibility made iteration faster, but the broader architecture was intentionally designed to support multiple asset classes and strategy types over time.  
The real long-term goal for me is building infrastructure and operational tooling around automated trading rather than pretending any single strategy is a magic solution.
